critique.summary: The article reports that Honda is discontinuing its electric vehicle (EV) programs, including halting development of new EV models and stopping production of the Prologue, a GM-built EV. The author argues this decision risks Honda falling behind in the automotive industry's shift toward electric drivetrains and software-defined vehicles, potentially threatening the company's future competitiveness.
mainPoints: Honda has stopped development of its electric Acura RDX, Honda 0 sedan and SUV, and will cease production of the Prologue EV.
The company blames U.S. tariffs and Chinese competition but lacked a strong EV strategy from the start.
Honda risks falling behind in key industry trends: electric drivetrains and software-defined vehicles (SDVs).
Developing EVs as original products rather than retrofitting internal combustion platforms is crucial for efficiency and cost.
Honda's retreat from EVs means missing out on important learning opportunities in development, manufacturing, and customer feedback.
The company faces an identity crisis as a traditional internal combustion engine maker while the market shifts to EVs and SDVs.
Honda's competitiveness is declining in China due to inability to offer EVs with better value than newer manufacturers, contributing to significant losses.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims align with publicly available information as of early 2026. Reports from Automotive News and Honda's own earnings statements confirm Honda's halt on key EV projects and production of the Prologue. The challenges cited, such as legacy automakers struggling with EV transitions and the importance of software-defined vehicles, are widely recognized in industry analysis. However, the article presents a strongly critical perspective and does not cite multiple sources or perspectives, limiting its journalistic breadth.
considerations: Honda's future EV strategy beyond the halted projects is not detailed; the company may pursue alternative approaches or partnerships.
The article does not explore Honda's hybrid or fuel cell vehicle efforts, which could be part of its broader electrification plan.
Economic and regulatory factors influencing Honda's decisions, such as changing government incentives or global supply chain issues, are not deeply examined.
The perspective of Honda executives or official company statements beyond earnings reports is limited, reducing insight into their strategic rationale.
Competitive dynamics in the EV market, including the role of other legacy automakers and new entrants, are not fully contextualized.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Honda's decision to scale back its electric vehicle (EV) efforts, with commenters debating the viability of EVs, software-defined vehicles, and market dynamics. Opinions vary on Honda's strategy, the future of EV adoption globally, and the challenges posed by battery production and infrastructure.

comments.author: dangus
comments.text:
I don’t think the title is hyperbole. Toyota isn’t giving up on their long term EV R&D plans.

Just look at Nissan, which is broke as a joke, but they still put a new Leaf model on the market.

Lately there’s been a vibe that the EV experiment has died off, but that really isn’t true looking at industry reporting.

There is stalling that seems related to subsidy expiration and/or scale back, but we could argue that subsidies expiring is happening because the subsidies aren’t needed to sell vehicles anymore.

20% of new vehicles sold globally are EVs. Critical mass has been achieved, and not just in China (20% of vehicles sold in Europe are EVs).

This is also an admission that Honda is just giving up on Acura completely. That $50k two row luxury SUV buyer that is such an industry staple buyer for the US auto industry is going to be buying Rivian R2s instead of an EV Acura MDX.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced and data-supported perspective on the EV market, highlighting global sales figures and industry trends.

comments.author: TrackerFF
comments.text:
I live in a top EV market, Norway.

ICE cars have been planned out for years now, and something like 96% of all new cars in Norway were EV last year.

Basically, if you plan on keeping selling ICE cars, you're removing yourself from the market here. There's no future for new personal ICE cars here.

I figure most other countries will be the same.
comments.analysis: The comment accurately reflects Norway's leading position in EV adoption and the declining market for ICE vehicles in that region.

comments.author: nytesky
comments.text:
Honda is an engine company at its heart. It makes very reliable, long lived engines.

They refine technology not really invent it (maybe invented VTEC). The transition to EV will be very gradual, I don’t even think we have enough rare earth metals and electrical grid capacity to go even twice as fast in adoption?

Honda is waiting for the standards and technology to settle out and become commodity technology, then they implement and iterate to a refined and reliable product.

It doesn’t seem like a winner take all market for EV? What would be the most? Perhaps I am ignorant on that part of market dynamics.

*edit for typos
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully addresses Honda's corporate identity and realistic challenges in EV adoption, emphasizing gradual transition.

comments.author: billfor
comments.text:
"Here, Honda is setting itself up for failure on the second disruption sweeping the automotive industry: the software-defined vehicle (SDV), which has core capabilities that can be upgraded and improved over time."

I'll pay triple for a non software defined vehicle that doesn't track me and can't be touched by the dealer once I purchase it. My one SDV (Tesla) is still on FSD from 2023 because the newer versions are terrible judging from the comments on the Tesla forums.
comments.analysis: Reflects a consumer perspective skeptical of software-defined vehicles, highlighting privacy and control concerns with some valid points.

comments.author: GianFabien
comments.text:
Smart doorbells and thermostats that upgraded in the night often became a nuisance or an expensive brick. But a faulty software upgrade on a car can kill you and others.

Car company execs need to take a chill pill followed by a reality serum. Monetizing subscription based basic features and delivering in-car advertising is the absolutely worst way to go.

As consumers we need to stop buying into the bells, whistles and trinkets and demand essential and safe transportation.
comments.analysis: Raises important safety and ethical concerns about software updates and monetization strategies in modern vehicles.

comments.author: bryanlarsen
comments.text:
OTOH, it really looks like Toyota is Goldilocks. Most companies invested too much too early and had to write off a substantial amount, but Toyota is rolling into 2027 with a small but nice selection of EV's.

Over 25% of vehicles sold world-wide were electric in 2025, and that percentage is steadily increasing. So VW & Ford were "too hot", Honda is looking like "too cold" and Toyota might be the "just right" of the three bears.
comments.analysis: Provides a comparative industry analysis with reasonable market share data, though some figures may be optimistic.

comments.author: mullingitover
comments.text:
I'm convinced that the Japanese government is terrified of EVs because all the small and medium-sized businesses which support the Japanese auto industry will be absolutely gutted when vehicles contain drastically fewer parts.

That, and Japan is deeply screwed if they go all-in on EVs and then China decides they shouldn't be allowed access to any more rare earths.
comments.analysis: Offers plausible geopolitical and economic reasons behind Japan's cautious EV approach, though speculative.

comments.author: sys_64738
comments.text: Anything you need to plug into a power source is doomed to fail. EVs are simply not designed properly which is why hybrids are the best of both world. A Camry hybrid has some genius technology as the EV part is used at low speed and ICE at higher speed. That is the perfect balance and you see why it's a success for them. Toyota make the best hybrid vehicles. Honda makes hybrids too so they're not throwing all their EV technology into the e-waste bin.
comments.analysis: Accurately highlights hybrid technology advantages and Toyota's leadership, providing a nuanced view on EV vs hybrid.

comments.author: gcanyon
comments.text:
EVs are going to be an extinction-level event for carmakers.

As the buggy-makers failed to transition to making cars, and thus ceased to be, so too will automakers fail to transition to EVs, and thus end their viability as vehicle manufacturers.
comments.analysis: Dramatic but valid warning about industry disruption due to EV transition, though somewhat hyperbolic.

comments.author: epolanski
comments.text:
I hate those narratives that if you don't jump on EVs, your future is doomed.

The last 5 years just don't show it. The EV market is still small and infrastructure missing in most of the world.

Toyota played it safe and made bank when everybody was saying they were doomed.

German automakers went hard on EVs. VW group sold 1 million fully electric vehicles in 2025, they will probably overtake Tesla in a couple of years for the biggest non-Chinese EV automaker by sales, but is it paying off financially?

At the same time german premium brands have a very hard time differentiating when Chinese cars offer similar quality at half the price even after tariffs.
comments.analysis: Provides a cautious and realistic counterpoint about EV adoption pace and infrastructure challenges worldwide.
